name: Severe Bleeding
first_aid_steps: Call emergency services immediately if bleeding is spurting or cannot be controlled.
Apply firm, direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth or sterile gauze.
If the cloth soaks through, do not remove it; place another cloth on top and continue pressure.
Maintain pressure until help arrives or the bleeding stops completely.
If the limb is affected and pressure fails, consider a professional tourniquet if trained.
red_flags: Blood spurting from the wound
Bleeding that does not stop after 10 minutes of direct pressure
Signs of shock (pale skin, rapid pulse, confusion)
Debris embedded in the wound
doctor_consult: Seek emergency medical care immediately for any uncontrolled or heavy bleeding.

name: Minor Cuts and Scrapes
first_aid_steps: Wash your hands thoroughly before touching the wound to prevent infection.
Rinse the wound with clean, running water to remove dirt and debris.
Apply a thin layer of antibiotic ointment or petroleum jelly.
Cover the area with a sterile bandage or gauze to keep it clean.
Change the bandage daily or whenever it becomes wet or dirty.
red_flags: Signs of infection (redness, warmth, pus, swelling)
The cut is deep enough to see fat or muscle
The wound was caused by a rusty or dirty object
doctor_consult: See a doctor if the wound shows signs of infection or if you haven't had a tetanus shot in over 10 years.
home_remedies: Honey (medical grade) for its antibacterial properties
Aloe vera to soothe the surrounding skin

name: Thermal Burns (First and Second Degree)
first_aid_steps: Cool the burn immediately under cool (not cold) running water for at least 10–20 minutes.
Remove jewelry or tight clothing near the burned area before swelling begins.
Do not break any blisters that may form.
Apply a light, loose sterile dressing to protect the skin.
Take over-the-counter pain relievers if needed for discomfort.
red_flags: Burn involves the face, hands, feet, or genitals
Skin appears charred, white, or leathery (3rd degree)
The burn is larger than 3 inches in diameter
doctor_consult: Consult a doctor for all second-degree burns or if the pain increases after 24 hours.
home_remedies: Aloe vera gel (pure) to soothe minor first-degree burns

name: Heat Stroke
first_aid_steps: Call emergency services immediately as this is a life-threatening emergency.
Move the person to a cool, shaded, or air-conditioned environment.
Cool the person quickly using a cool bath, spray with a hose, or damp sheets.
Place ice packs or cool wet towels on the neck, armpits, and groin.
Do not give the person fluids if they are unconscious or vomiting.
red_flags: Body temperature above 104°F (40°C)
Confusion, agitation, or slurred speech
Loss of consciousness or seizures
Hot, dry skin or heavy sweating
doctor_consult: Always require immediate emergency hospital treatment for suspected heat stroke.

name: Snake Bite
first_aid_steps: Move away from the snake's striking distance and call emergency services.
Keep the person calm and still to slow the spread of venom.
Keep the bitten limb at or slightly below the level of the heart.
Clean the wound gently with soap and water; do not flush with high pressure.
Remove any rings or restrictive clothing, as the area will likely swell.
red_flags: Fang marks
Severe pain and swelling at the site
Difficulty breathing
Nausea, vomiting, or blurred vision
doctor_consult: Seek emergency care immediately; do not wait for symptoms to appear. Do not use a tourniquet or attempt to suck out venom.

name: Insect Sting
first_aid_steps: Remove the stinger by scraping it with a flat object like a credit card (don't use tweezers).
Wash the area with soap and water.
Apply a cold pack to reduce pain and swelling.
Elevate the affected limb if possible.
Monitor for signs of a severe allergic reaction (anaphylaxis).
red_flags: Difficulty breathing or wheezing
Swelling of the lips, tongue, or throat
Hives or a widespread rash
Faintness or dizziness
doctor_consult: Call emergency services immediately if the person has a known allergy or shows signs of anaphylaxis.
home_remedies: Baking soda paste to neutralize acidity

name: Choking (Adult/Child)
first_aid_steps: Ask 'Are you choking?' If they can cough or speak, encourage them to keep coughing.
If they cannot breathe, give 5 back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
If the object isn't dislodged, perform 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver).
Repeat the cycle of 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object is forced out.
If the person becomes unconscious, lower them to the ground and start CPR.
red_flags: Inability to speak, cry, or cough forcefully
Skin turning blue (cyanosis)
Clutched throat (the universal sign of choking)
doctor_consult: Seek medical evaluation after the object is cleared to ensure no internal damage or remaining fragments.

name: Fainting (Syncope)
first_aid_steps: Lay the person down on their back.
Elevate the person's legs about 12 inches above heart level if there are no injuries.
Loosen belts, collars, or other restrictive clothing.
Check for breathing; if they aren't breathing, begin CPR and call 911.
If they fainted due to heat, move them to a cool place and offer sips of water when conscious.
red_flags: Fainting associated with chest pain
The person does not regain consciousness within one minute
Fainting while sitting or lying down
Irregular heartbeat
doctor_consult: See a doctor to rule out underlying heart issues, especially if fainting occurs during exercise.
home_remedies: Drinking water and electrolyte fluids if caused by dehydration

name: Electric Shock
first_aid_steps: Do not touch the person if they are still in contact with the electrical source.
Turn off the source of electricity if possible, or move the source away using a dry wooden stick.
Call emergency services immediately.
Check for breathing and a pulse; start CPR if necessary.
Cover any burned areas with a sterile gauze bandage.
red_flags: Cardiac arrest or irregular heart rhythm
Severe burns at entry and exit points
Muscle pain or contractions
Seizures
doctor_consult: All victims of significant electric shock should be evaluated in an emergency room, as internal damage may not be visible.

name: Sprains and Fractures
first_aid_steps: Rest the injured limb and avoid putting weight on it.
Ice the area using a cold pack wrapped in a towel for 15–20 minutes at a time.
Compress the injury with an elastic bandage to reduce swelling.
Elevate the injured part above the level of the heart.
Splint the limb in the position found if you must move the person.
red_flags: Limb is visibly deformed or out of place
Bone is protruding through the skin
Inability to move the joint or bear weight
Numbness or tingling in the injured area
doctor_consult: Consult a doctor for an X-ray if there is severe pain, deformity, or inability to walk.
home_remedies: Epsom salt soak (only after the first 48 hours) to reduce residual muscle soreness

name: Food Poisoning
first_aid_steps: Stop eating and drinking for a few hours to let the stomach settle.
Sip small amounts of water, clear broth, or electrolyte drinks to stay hydrated.
Gradually reintroduce bland foods like crackers, toast, or rice.
Avoid dairy, caffeine, alcohol, and fatty foods until symptoms pass.
Rest as much as possible to allow the body to recover.
red_flags: High fever (above 102°F)
Bloody stools or vomit
Frequent vomiting that prevents keeping liquids down
Signs of severe dehydration (no urination, extreme thirst)
doctor_consult: Seek medical help if symptoms last more than 3 days or if the patient is an infant or elderly.
